dublinCore.dc:title: 119 S4809 IS: Venezuela Democratic Transition Act
dublinCore.dc:rights: Pursuant to Title 17 Section 105 of the United States Code, this file is not subject to copyright protection and is in the public domain.

form.current-chamber: IN THE SENATE OF THE UNITED STATES

action.action-desc: Mr. Kaine introduced the following bill; which was read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations

form.official-title: To require the Secretary of State to develop a strategy for supporting free and fair elections in Venezuela, to impose sanctions on individuals who are complicit in gross violations of internationally recognized human rights in Venezuela, and for other purposes.

section.text: This Act may be cited as the Venezuela Democratic Transition Act.

paragraph.text: the Venezuelan parliamentary election held on December 6, 2020, and the Venezuelan parliamentary election held on May 25, 2025, were fraudulent and widely condemned;
the Venezuelan presidential election held on July 28, 2024, violated Venezuelan law and international standards;

the Venezuelan regime led by Nicolás Maduro falsely claimed victory in the 2024 presidential election and intensified violent repression against the leaders and supporters of the opposition presidential candidate; and
Nicolás Maduro lost the 2024 presidential election and his regime lacks legitimacy to represent the Venezuelan people.

subsection.text: It is the sense of Congress that the United States should—

paragraph.text: continue to recognize Edmundo González Urrutia as Venezuela’s legitimate President-elect;
support the democratic opposition;
facilitate free and fair presidential elections in 2026 with full opposition participation; and
ensure that any energy agreements with Venezuela are made only in consultation with the democratic opposition movement.
The terms admission, admitted, and alien have the meanings given such terms in section 101 of the Immigration and Nationality Act (8 U.S.C. 1101).
The term appropriate congressional committees means—

subparagraph.text: the Committee on Foreign Relations of the Senate;
the Committee on the Judiciary of the Senate;
the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs of the Senate;
the Committee on Foreign Affairs of the House of Representatives;
the Committee on the Judiciary of the House of Representatives; and
the Committee on Financial Services of the House of Representatives.

paragraph.header: Gross violations of internationally recognized human rights
paragraph.text: The term gross violations of internationally recognized human rights has the meaning given that term in section 502B(d) of the Foreign Assistance Act of 1961 (22 U.S.C. 2304(d)).
The term United States person means—

subparagraph.text: a United States citizen or an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence to the United States;
an entity organized under the laws of the United States or any jurisdiction within the United States, including a foreign branch of such an entity; or
any person located in the United States.

paragraph.text: The term Venezuelan person means—

subparagraph.text: a citizen or national of Venezuela; or
an entity organized under the laws of Venezuela or otherwise subject to the jurisdiction of the Government of Venezuela.

section.header: Strategy for supporting free and fair elections in Venezuela
section.text: The Secretary of State shall develop and submit to Congress a strategy that—

paragraph.text: updates, with input from the appropriate congressional committees, the Democratic Transition Framework for Venezuela, which was originally published by the Department of State on March 31, 2020;
uses all available diplomatic tools—

subparagraph.text: to facilitate a new presidential election in Venezuela in 2026 that complies with international standards for a free, fair, and transparent electoral process;
to end the Maduro-Rodriguez regime’s usurpation of presidential authorities;
to restore democracy and the rule of law in Venezuela;
to free political prisoners and prisoners of conscience from incarceration in Venezuela; and
to facilitate the consistent delivery of humanitarian assistance to the people of Venezuela;

paragraph.text: outlines benchmarks towards a democratic transition in Venezuela;
provides a detailed assessment of Venezuelan government institutions, political prisoners, detention and torture centers, armed groups, and laws used to repress political opposition;
outlines the next steps that need to be taken in Venezuela—

subparagraph.text: to coordinate international sanctions;
to close torture centers;
to ensure the Venezuelan military respects the results of free and fair presidential elections;
to establish mechanisms for institutional reform;
to guarantee the safe return of opposition leaders, including María Corina Machado; and
to provide pathways for transitional justice and accountability; and

paragraph.text: requires transparency and monthly reporting to the appropriate congressional committees with respect to the transition to democracy in Venezuela, including—

subparagraph.text: closely monitoring energy-related negotiations; and
tracking progress made toward achieving certain democratic benchmarks.

section.header: Imposition of sanctions with respect to human rights violations in Venezuela

subsection.text: The President shall impose the sanctions described in subsection (b) with respect to Venezuelan persons the President determines are complicit in gross violations of internationally recognized human rights in Venezuela.
The sanctions described in this subsection are the following:

paragraph.text: The President shall exercise all of the powers granted by the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (50 U.S.C. 1701 et seq.) to the extent necessary to block and prohibit all transactions in all property and interests in property of a person described in subsection (a), if such property and interests in property are in the United States, come within the United States, or are or come within the procession or control of a United States person.

paragraph.header: Aliens inadmissible for visas, admission, or parole
paragraph.text: In the case of an alien described in subsection (a), the alien is—

subparagraph.text: inadmissible to the United States;
ineligible for a visa or other documentation to enter the United States; and
otherwise ineligible to be admitted or paroled into the United States or to receive any other benefit under the Immigration and Nationality Act (8 U.S.C. 1101 et seq.).

paragraph.header: Exception relating to importation of goods

subparagraph.text: A requirement to block and prohibit all transactions in all property and interests in property under this section shall not include the authority or a requirement to impose sanctions on the importation of goods.
In this paragraph, the term good means any article, natural or manmade substance, material, supply, or manufactured product, including inspection and test equipment, and excluding technical data.

paragraph.header: Exception to comply with united nations headquarters agreement and law enforcement activities
paragraph.text: Sanctions under subsection (b)(2) shall not apply with respect to the admission of an alien to the United States if admitting or paroling the alien into the United States is necessary—

subparagraph.text: to permit the United States to comply with the Agreement regarding the Headquarters of the United Nations, signed at Lake Success June 26, 1947, and entered into force November 21, 1947, between the United Nations and the United States, or other applicable international obligations of the United States; or
to carry out or assist authorized law enforcement activity in the United States.

paragraph.header: Exception to comply with intelligence activities
paragraph.text: Sanctions under this section shall not apply to any activity subject to the reporting requirements under title V of the National Security Act of 1947 (50 U.S.C. 3091 et seq.) or any authorized intelligence activities of the United States.
The President may waive the application of sanctions under this section if the President—

subparagraph.text: determines such a waiver is in the national security interests of the United States; and
submits to the appropriate congressional committees a report on the waiver and the reasons for the waiver.
Sanctions under this section shall not apply to—

clause.text: the conduct or facilitation of a transaction for the provision of agricultural commodities, food, medicine, medical devices, humanitarian assistance, or for humanitarian purposes; or
transactions that are necessary for or related to the activities described in clause (i).

subparagraph.text: In this paragraph:

clause.text: The term agricultural commodity has the meaning given that term in section 102 of the Agricultural Trade Act of 1978 (7 U.S.C. 5602).
The term medical device has the meaning given the term device in section 201 of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (21 U.S.C. 321).
The term medicine has the meaning given the term drug in section 201 of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (21 U.S.C. 321).

paragraph.text: The President may waive the application of sanctions under this section upon receiving certifications from the Secretary of State and the Secretary of the Treasury that a democratic transition has occurred in Venezuela.
The President may exercise all authorities provided under sections 203 and 205 of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (50 U.S.C. 1702 and 1704) to carry out this section.

A person that violates, attempts to violate, conspires to violate, or causes a violation of this section or any regulation, license, or order issued to carry out this section shall be subject to the penalties set forth in subsections (b) and (c) of section 206 of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (50 U.S.C. 1705) to the same extent as a person that commits an unlawful act described in subsection (a) of that section.
